Cultural Significance: Major crop in India Mexico and Mediterranean; used in hummus and curries
Culinary Uses: Chickpeas; hummus, falafel, curries, salads, roasted snacks

Cultural Significance: Foundation of East Asian cuisine (tofu soy sauce miso)
Culinary Uses: Soybeans; tofu, soy milk, tempeh, miso, soy sauce, edamame

Cultural Significance: Most produced vegetable oil; neutral flavor; Asian cooking
Culinary Uses: Soybean oil; cooking oil, margarine, salad dressings

Cultural Significance: Drought-resistant; used in soups and as animal feed.
Drought-resistant; important famine food.
Staple in Middle Eastern and Indian cuisine; high protein and iron
Culinary Uses: Lentils; soups, stews, dal, salads, Indian cuisine

Cultural Significance: Bitter seeds used as condiment in Oaxacan *salsa de guaje*; high in protein but contains mimosine (toxic in excess).
Culinary Uses: Guaje; seeds in salsas and moles; pods cooked; limited consumption

Cultural Significance: Mediterranean legume; requires soaking to remove alkaloids.
Nutrient-dense "miracle tree"; leaves pods and seeds all edible
Culinary Uses: Drumstick tree; leaves as greens, pods in curries, seeds for water purification

Cultural Significance: Traditional medicine; food after processing; tropical cover crop
Fermented seeds used as umami condiment (dawadawa) in West African stews
Large speckled bean; essential for *frijoles charros* and *pozole*; frost-tolerant highland variety.
Culinary Uses: Runner beans; British cuisine, stews, Mexican dishes

Cultural Significance: Staple in Latin American and Andean cuisine; large flat seeds
Staple protein source; often eaten with maize (Three Sisters agriculture)
Culinary Uses: Common beans; soups, stews, baked beans, refried beans, chili

Regional Names: Flor de Mayo (Mexico); May Flower Bean (Global)
Cultural Significance: Mexican landrace with pink mottled seeds; cooks quickly without soaking; staple in northern Mexico.
Culinary Uses: Common beans; soups, stews, baked beans, refried beans, chili

Cultural Significance: Oaxacan heirloom bean; small black seeds; essential for *mole negro* accompaniments.
Culinary Uses: Common beans; soups, stews, baked beans, refried beans, chili

Cultural Significance: Sweet or field pea; European gardens; split pea soups
Culinary Uses: Peas; soups, stews, side dishes, salads, split pea soup

Scientific Author: (Humb. & Bonpl. ex Willd.) M. C. Johnst.
Cultural Significance: Sweet pods ground into flour; pre-Hispanic sweetener; drought-tolerant desert tree.
Culinary Uses: Mesquite; pods ground to flour, sweetener; traditional Mexican cuisine

Regional Names: Kacang Bendo (Indonesia); Goa Bean (Global); Asparagus Pea (UK)
Cultural Significance: All parts edible (pods leaves flowers tubers); high protein content
Edible tubers and seeds; important in Nigeria.
Ancient crop; used in falafel ful medames and European stews
Culinary Uses: Fava beans; Mediterranean cuisine, stews, falafel (Egyptian), salads

Cultural Significance: Essential for dosa idli and papad in Indian cuisine
Culinary Uses: Urad dal; Indian cuisine, papadums, idli, dosa

Cultural Significance: Used for sprouts dal and glass noodles; fast-sprouting
Culinary Uses: Mung beans; sprouts, dal, desserts, Asian cuisine

Cultural Significance: Drought-resistant legume; important food security crop in Sahel
Drought tolerant legume; vital for soil fertility and human nutrition in Sahel
Culinary Uses: Cowpeas/black-eyed peas; Hoppin' John, stews, salads, African cuisine

Cultural Significance: High-protein, drought-resistant; used in soups and as a snack.
Pre-Hispanic supergreen; high in calcium and iron; used in *guisados* and *revoltijo*.
Culinary Uses: Amaranth greens; cooked as vegetable; seeds as grain alternative

Cultural Significance: Colorful leafy; Caribbean and Asian cuisines
Culinary Uses: Chinese spinach; stir-fried, in soups, salads

Cultural Significance: Tropical vine; mucilaginous leaves used in coastal Karnataka curries (Basale Gassi)
Culinary Uses: Malabar spinach; stir-fried, in soups, as thickener

Cultural Significance: Trendy superfood; traditional winter green in Europe.
Culinary Uses: Cabbage (fermented as sauerkraut, kimchi), kale, broccoli, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts

Cultural Significance: Cultivar of wild cabbage; developed in Italy; high in sulforaphane
Culinary Uses: Broccoli; steamed, roasted, stir-fried, in salads, soups

Cultural Significance: Trendy superfood; traditional winter green in Europe; cold-hardy
Culinary Uses: Collard greens; braised, in Southern US cuisine, Portuguese soup

Cultural Significance: Pre-Hispanic grain-vegetable; flower heads battered and fried; used in *revoltijo*.
Culinary Uses: Wild quinoa; seeds cooked as grain, leaves as greens

Cultural Significance: Edible chrysanthemum; Japanese and Chinese hot pots
Culinary Uses: Garland chrysanthemum; Japanese/Chinese cuisine, hot pot, salads

Cultural Significance: Maya supergreen; must be cooked to neutralize cyanogenic glycosides; higher protein than spinach.
Culinary Uses: Chaya; cooked as greens (must be cooked to remove toxins), in soups, tamales

Cultural Significance: Leafy green used in soups and tamales.
Culinary Uses: Chipilín; cooked greens, in tamales, soups, Central American cuisine

Cultural Significance: Himalayan and North American spring delicacy; must be cooked to remove toxins
Culinary Uses: Fiddlehead fern; steamed, stir-fried, in salads, Asian cuisine

Cultural Significance: Wild greens harvested from corn fields; represents milpa agroecosystem biodiversity.
Culinary Uses: Tomatillo; salsas (salsa verde), sauces, Mexican cuisine

Cultural Significance: Strong cilantro-anise flavor; essential in *tlacoyos* and tacos; does not dry well.
Culinary Uses: Yacon; eaten raw like fruit, syrup; leaves for tea

Cultural Significance: Origin Persia; iron-rich leafy green; used globally in salads and cooked dishes
Culinary Uses: Cooked as greens, raw in salads, spanakopita, creamed spinach, smoothies

Cultural Significance: Essential for Lenten *revoltijo* with mole and potatoes; grows in saline soils.
Culinary Uses: Romero; cooked in Lenten dishes (revoltijo), salads
